channel.title: Home on Alan Aw
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Alan Aw

item.title: Review of “Who We Are and How We Got Here"
item.description: One of my goals this year is to read more books and to think critically about them. I’ve started a spreadsheet tracking books I’ve read and books I’m reading, and I’m excited to share it here. I just finished reading David Reich’s book on ancient DNA (aDNA) and human history, Reich, David. Who We Are and How We Got Here: Ancient DNA and the new science of the human past.

item.title: Lagrange Multipliers Revisited
item.description: Happy New Year! This is my first post, and I intend to use this space to write about my thoughts and learning. Any constructive feedback would be appreciated! Now for the actual post. I’ve been thinking about mathematics over Winter break, mostly because I often find myself overlooking details about important results and consequently learning maths not as thoroughly as I’d like. I find this especially true with regard to analysis (or continuous maths in general), so I try to revisit the maths every now and then.

About Me code{white-space: pre;} pre:not([class]) { background-color: white; } if (window.hljs) { hljs.configure({languages: []}); hljs.initHighlightingOnLoad(); if (document.readyState && document.readyState === "complete") { window.setTimeout(function() { hljs.initHighlighting(); }, 0); } } h1 { font-size: 34px; } h1.title { font-size: 38px; } h2 { font-size: 30px; } h3 { font-size: 24px; } h4 { font-size: 18px; } h5 { font-size: 16px; } h6 { font-size: 12px; } .

item.title: About Me
item.description: I am a PhD student in the Department of Statistics at the University of California at Berkeley. I work in the labs of Yun Song and Nilah Ioannidis, where I build statistical models and methods to analyze genomic and single-cell chromatin data. I completed my undergraduate studies in Mathematical and Computational Science (with a Biology minor) at Stanford University, where I was advised by Bradley Efron. I was fortunate to do research in mathematical and computational biology with Noah Rosenberg, Marcus Feldman and Julia Palacios.

item.title: Research
item.description: I mainly develop approaches to (i) detect stratification in human populations and to (ii) utilize such stratification information to address downstream tasks like computing polygenic scores and fine-mapping. I describe my main research themes below, and emphasize ongoing work (2021-present) with ❗. Exchangeability and Population Stratification (A) Performing PCA on genotypes that are drawn from a panmictic population. (B) Falsely detecting ancestrally informative SNPs when the samples were drawn from a panmictic population

item.title: Stat 135 (Spring 2019)
item.description: I served as a GSI for this upper division undergraduate course providing a first introduction to mathematical statistics. Based on problems provided by the Instructor Adam Lucas, I prepared and used slides to facilitate team work during section. I also occasionally wrote notes for students to supplement my in-class teaching. Here is a thematic summary I typed up for students to strategize for their final exam review. Week 1: Introduction Week 2: Sampling, parametric models / R setup and practice Week 3: MoM estimation / bootstrap practice [slides] Week 4: Maximum likelihood estimation, CRLB [slides] Week 5: Sufficiency [slides] Week 6: Hypothesis testing, Neyman-Pearson lemma, duality of HT and CI [slides] [notes] Week 7: Comparing two samples: parametric Week 8: (Midterm review) [jeopardy slides] Week 9: Likelihood ratio test, UMP test, GLRT [slides] Week 10: (Spring break) Week 11: Comparing two samples: non-parametric, chi-square tests Week 12: ANOVA Week 13: Linear model (single predictor), least squares [notes] Week 14: Linear model (multiple predictors) Week 15: Bayesian statistics [slides]

item.title: Teaching
item.description: Teaching Philosophy In 2019, I wrote a statement of teaching philosophy as part of a statistics teaching seminar, which you can read here. Look below for courses I have taught and feel free to click around for material. Past Stat 153: Introduction to Time Series Analysis (Instructor: Jared Fisher) Stat 135: Concepts of Statistics (Instructor: Adam Lucas) [material]
